en.extract:
The brown thrasher (Toxostoma rufum), sometimes erroneously called the brown thrush or fox-coloured thrush, is a bird in the family Mimidae, which also includes the New World catbirds and mockingbirds. The brown thrasher is abundant throughout the eastern and central United States and southern and central Canada, and it is the only thrasher to live primarily east of the Rockies and central Texas. It is the state bird of Georgia.
As a member of the genus Toxostoma, the bird is relatively large-sized among the other thrashers. It has brown upper parts with a white under part with dark streaks. Because of this, it is often confused with the smaller wood thrush (Hylocichla mustelina), among other species. The brown thrasher is noted for having over 1000 song types, and the largest song repertoire of birds. However, each note is usually repeated in two or three phrases.
The brown thrasher is an omnivore, with its diet ranging from insects to fruits and nuts. The usual nesting areas are shrubs, small trees, or at times on ground level. Brown thrashers are generally inconspicuous but territorial birds, especially when defending their nests, and will attack species as large as humans.


Taxonomy and naming
The brown thrasher was originally described by Carl Linnaeus in his landmark 1758 10th edition of Systema Naturae as Turdus rufus. The genus name Toxostoma comes from the Ancient Greek toxon, "bow" or "arch" and stoma, "mouth". The specific rufum is Latin for "red", but covers a wider range of hues than the English term.
Although not in the thrush family, this bird is sometimes erroneously called the brown thrush. The name misconception could be because the word thrasher is believed to derive from the word thrush. The naturalist Mark Catesby called it the fox-coloured thrush.
Genetic studies have found that the brown thrasher is most closely related to the long-billed and Cozumel thrashers (T. longirostre & guttatum), within the genus Toxostoma.


Description
The brown thrasher is bright reddish-brown above with thin, dark streaks on its buffy underparts. It has a whitish-colored chest with distinguished teardrop-shaped markings on its chest. Its long, rufous tail is rounded with paler corners, and eyes are a brilliant yellow. Its bill is brownish, long, and curves downward. Both male and females are similar in appearance. The juvenile appearance of the brown thrasher from the adult is not remarkably different, except for plumage texture, indiscreet upper part markings, and the irises having an olive color.

The brown thrasher is a fairly large passerine, although it is generally moderate in size for a thrasher, being distinctly larger than the sage thrasher (Oreoscoptes montanus) but similar or somewhat smaller in size than the more brownish Toxostoma species found further west. Adults measure around 23.5 to 30.5 cm (9.3 to 12.0 in) long with a wingspan of 29 to 33 cm (11 to 13 in), and weigh 61 to 89 g (2.2 to 3.1 oz), with an average of 68 g (2.4 oz). Among standard measurements, the wing chord is 9.5 to 11.5 cm (3.7 to 4.5 in), the tail is 10.9 to 14.1 cm (4.3 to 5.6 in), the culmen is 2.2 to 2.9 cm (0.87 to 1.14 in) and the tarsus is 3.2 to 3.6 cm (1.3 to 1.4 in). There are two subspecies: the 'brown thrasher' (T. rufum rufum), which lies in the eastern half of Canada and the United States, and the 'western brown thrasher' (T. rufum longicauda (Baird, 1858)), which resides in the central United States east of the Rocky Mountains and southern central Canada. The western brown thrasher is distinguished by a more cinnamon upper part, whiter wing bars, and darker breast spots than T.rufum rufum.
The lifespan of the brown thrasher varies on a year-to-year basis, as the rate of survival the first year is 35%, 50% in between the second and third year, and 75% between the third and fourth year. Disease and exposure to cold weather are among contributing factors for the limits of the lifespan. However, the longest lived thrasher in the wild is 12 years, and relatively the same for ones in captivity.


Similar species
The similar-looking long-billed thrasher has a significantly smaller range. It has a gray head and neck, and has a longer bill than the brown thrasher. The brown thrasher's appearance is also strikingly similar to the wood thrush, the bird that it is usually mistaken for. However, the wood thrush has dark rounded spots on its under parts rather than the brown thrashers' elongated streaks, has dark eyes, shorter tail, a shorter, straighter bill (with the head generally more typical of a thrush), and is a smaller bird.


Distribution and habitat

The brown thrasher resides in various habitats. It prefers to live in woodland edges, thickets and dense brush, often searching for food in dry leaves on the ground. It can also inhabit areas that are agricultural and near suburban areas, but is less likely to live near housing than other bird species. The brown thrasher often vies for habitat and potential nesting grounds with other birds, which is usually initiated by the males.
The brown thrasher is a strong, but partial migrant, as the bird is a year-round resident in the southern portion of its range. The breeding range includes the United States and Canada east of the Rocky Mountains, but has been occasionally spotted West of the Rockies. The increase in trees throughout the Great Plains during the past century due to fire suppression and tree planting facilitated a westward range expansion of the brown thrasher as well as range expansions of many other species of birds. Studies indicate that thrashers that reside in the New England region of the United States during the breeding season fly toward the Carolinas and Georgia, birds located in the east of the Mississippi winter from Arkansas to Georgia, and birds located in the Dakotas and the central Canadian provinces head towards eastern Texas and Louisiana. When the species does migrate, it is typically for short distances and during the night. There are also records of the bird wintering in Mexico, as well as a British record of a transatlantic vagrant.


Behavior

The brown thrasher has been observed either solo or in pairs. The brown thrasher is usually an elusive bird, and maintains its evasiveness with low-level flying. When it feels bothered, it usually hides into thickets and gives cackling calls. Thrashers spend most of their time on ground level or near it. When seen, it is commonly the males that are singing from unadorned branches. The brown thrasher has been noted for having an aggressive behavior, and is a staunch defender of its nest. However, the name does not come from attacking perceived threats, but is believed to have come from the thrashing sound the bird makes when digging through ground debris. It is also thought that the name comes from the thrashing sound that is made while it is smashing large insects to kill and eventually eat.


Feeding
This bird is omnivorous, which has a diet that includes insects, berries, nuts and seeds, as well as earthworms, snails, and sometimes lizards and frogs. Across seasons and its breeding range, it was found 63% of stomach contents were made of animal matter, the remaining 37% being plant material. During the breeding season, the diet consists primarily of beetles, grasshoppers, and other arthropods, and fruits, nuts and seeds. More than 80% of the diet of brown thrasher from Illinois is made of animal matter, about 50% being beetles. In Iowa, about 20% of the summer diet was found to consist of grasshoppers. By the late summer, it begins to shift towards more of a herbivore diet, focusing on fruits, nuts, seeds, and grains, 60% of the food in Illinois being fruits and seeds. By winter, the customary diet of the brown thrasher is fruit and acorns. Wintering birds in Texas were found to eat 58% plant material (mainly sugar berry and poison ivy) and 42% animal material in October; by March, in the dry period when food supply is generally lower, 80% of the food became animal and only 20% plants. Vertebrates are only eaten occasionally and are often comprised by small reptiles and amphibians, such as lizards, small or young snakes, tree frogs and salamanders.
The brown thrasher utilizes its vision while scouring for food. It usually forages for food under leaves, brushes, and soil debris on the ground using its bill. It then swipes the floor in side-to-side motions, and investigates the area it recently foraged in. The brown thrasher forages in a similar method to the long-billed thrasher and Bendire's thrasher (T. longirostre & bendirei), picking food off the ground and under leaf litter, whereas thrashers with sharply decurved bills are more likely to dig into the ground to obtain food. Foraging success is 25% greater in dry leaf litter as compared to damp leaf letter. The brown thrasher can also hammer nuts such as acorns in order to remove the shell. It has also been noted for its flexibility in catching quick insects, as the amount of vertebrae in its neck exceeds giraffes and camels. In one case, a brown thrasher was observed to dig a hole about 1.5 cm (0.59 in) deep, place an acorn in it and hit the acorn until it cracked, considered to be a form of tool usage. In a laboratory experiment, a brown thrasher was found to be able to discern and reject the toxic eastern newt (Notophthalmus viridescens) and a palatable mimic of that species, the red salamander (Pseudotriton ruber), but continued to eat palatable dusky salamanders (Desmognathus spp.).


Breeding

Brown thrashers are typically monogamous birds, but mate-switching does occur, at times during the same season. Their breeding season varies by region. In the southeastern United States, the breeding months begin in February and March, while May and June see the commencement of breeding in the northern portion of their breeding range. When males enter the breeding grounds, their territory can range from 2 to 10 acres (0.81 to 4.05 ha). Around this time of the year the males are usually at their most active, singing loudly to attract potential mates, and are found on top of perches. The courting ritual involves the exchanging of probable nesting material. Males will sing gentler as they sight a female, and this enacts the female to grab a twig or leaf and present it to the male, with flapping wings and chirping sounds. The males might also present a gift in response and approach the female. Both sexes will take part in nest building once mates find each other, and will mate after the nest is completed.
The female lays 3 to 5 eggs, that usually appears with a blueish or greenish tint along with reddish-brown spots. There are rare occurrences of no spots on the eggs. The nest is built twiggy, lined with grass, leaves, and other forms of dead vegetation. The nests are typically built in a dense shrub or low in a tree, usually up to 2.1 m (6.9 ft) high, but have built nests as high as 6 m (20 ft). They also on occasion build nests on the ground. Between eleven days to two weeks, the eggs hatch. Both parents incubate and feed the young, with the female doing most of the incubating. Nine to thirteen days after hatching, the nestlings begin to fledge. These birds raise two, sometimes even three, broods in a year. The male sings a series of short repeated melodious phrases from an open perch to declare his territory, and is also very aggressive in defending the nest, known to strike people and animals.


Vocal development

The male brown thrasher may have the largest song repertoire of any North American bird, which has been documented as at least over 1,100 songs. Some sources state that each individual has up to 3,000 song phrases, while others put the number beyond 3,000. The males' singing voice usually contains more of a melodic tone than that of the related grey catbird. Its song are coherent phrases that are iterated no more than three times, but has been done for minutes at a time. By the fall, the male sings with smoother sub-songs. During the winter, the males may also sing in short spurts during altercations with neighboring males.
In the birds' youth, alarm noises are the sounds made. As an adult, the brown thrasher has an array of sounds it will make in various situations. Both male and females make smack and teeooo-like alarm calls when provoked, and hijjj sounds at dusk and dawn. Others calls may consist of an acute, sudden chakk, rrrrr, a Tcheh sound in the beginning that ends with an eeeur, kakaka, and sounds reminiscent of a stick scraping a concrete sidewalk. Brown thrashers are noted for their mimicry (as a member of the family Mimidae), but they are not as diverse in this category as their relative the northern mockingbird. However, during the breeding season, the mimicking ability of the male is at its best display, impersonating sounds from tufted titmice (Baeolophus bicolor), northern cardinals (Cardinalis cardinalis), wood thrushes, northern flickers (Colaptes auratus), among other species.


Predation and threats
Although this bird is widespread and still common, it has declined in numbers in some areas due to loss of suitable habitat. Despite the decrease, the rate does not warrant a status towards vulnerable. One of the natural nuisances is the parasitic brown-headed cowbird (Molothrus ater), but these incidents are rare. Whenever these situations occur, the brown thrashers usually discard the cowbirds' eggs. Occasionally, the thrasher has thrown out their own eggs instead of the cowbird eggs due to similar egg size, and at least one recorded event raised a fledgling. Northern cardinals and grey catbirds are also major competitors for thrashers in terms of territorial gain. Because of the apparent lack of opportunistic behavior around species like these, thrashers are prone to be driven out of zones for territory competition. Brown thrashers have tendencies to double-brood or have failures on their first nesting attempts due to predation. Grey catbirds have been seen invading brown thrashers' nests and breaking their eggs. Other than the catbird, snakes, birds of prey, and cats are among the top predators of the thrasher. In Kansas, at least eight species of snake were identified as potentially serious sources of nest failure. Among the identified avian predators of adults are Cooper's hawks (Accipiter cooperii), northern goshawk (Accipiter gentilis), broad-winged hawks (Buteo platypterus), merlins (Falco columbarius), peregrine falcons (Falco peregrinus), eastern screech-owls (Megascops asio), great horned owls (Bubo virginianus) barred owls (Strix varia) and long-eared owls (Asio otus).
The brown thrasher methods of defending itself include using its bill, which can inflict significant damage to species smaller than it, along with wing-flapping and vocal expressions.


State bird
The brown thrasher is the state bird of Georgia. The brown thrasher also was the inspiration for the name of Atlanta's former National Hockey League team, the Atlanta Thrashers, who relocated in 2011 to become the current Winnipeg Jets (the original Jets relocated to Phoenix in 1996 to become the Coyotes). The Coyotes have since relocated again and are now known as the Utah Mammoth.

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Toxostoma rufum -Garland, Texas, USA-8.jpg|thumb|In Texas, U.S.]]
The brown thrasher resides in various habitats. It prefers to live in woodland edges, thickets and dense brush,<ref name=Alberta92/> often searching for food in dry leaves on the ground.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://legacy.myfwc.com/bba/docs/bba_BRTH.pdf|title=Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission: A collaborative study of Florida's birdlife.|access-date=21 July 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140509001445/http://legacy.myfwc.com/bba/docs/bba_BRTH.pdf|archive-date=9 May 2014}}</ref> It can also inhabit areas that are agricultural and near suburban areas, but is less likely to live near housing than other bird species.<ref name=helm01/><ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> The brown thrasher often vies for habitat and potential nesting grounds with other birds, which is usually initiated by the males.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/>

The brown thrasher is a strong, but partial migrant, as the bird is a year-round resident in the [[Deep South|southern portion]] of its range.<ref name=Mobley09/> The breeding range includes the United States and Canada east of the Rocky Mountains, but has been occasionally spotted West of the Rockies.<ref>{{cite book|last=Patten|first=Michael A.|author2= McCaskie, Guy |author3=Unitt, Philipp|title=Birds of the Salton Sea: Status, Biogeography, and Ecology|publisher=University of California Press|year=2003|page=257|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=a73tq1dXqYcC&pg=PA257|isbn=978-0-520-23593-9}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|chapter-url=http://americanornithology.org/sites/default/files/files/imce-img/AOUchecklistPass-Mimidae.pdf|year=1998|publisher=American Ornithologists' Union|title=AOU Checklist of North American Birds|chapter=Passeriformes: Incertae Sedis – Mimidae. ''Schiffornis turdinus'' (Wied). Thrush-like ''Schiffornis''|page=416|access-date=2015-05-13|archive-date=2015-11-18|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151118161854/http://americanornithology.org/sites/default/files/files/imce-img/AOUchecklistPass-Mimidae.pdf}}</ref> The increase in trees throughout the Great Plains during the past century due to fire suppression and tree planting facilitated a westward range expansion of the brown thrasher<ref name="Cornell">Cavitt, J. F. and C. A. Haas (2014). Brown Thrasher (''Toxostoma rufum''). In ''The Birds of North America''. A. F. Poole (ed.) Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. {{doi|10.2173/bna.557}}</ref> as well as range expansions of many other species of birds.<ref>{{cite journal | author = Livezey KB | year = 2009a | title = Range expansion of Barred Owls, part I: chronology and distribution | journal = American Midland Naturalist | volume = 161 | pages = 49–56 | doi=10.1674/0003-0031-161.1.49| s2cid = 86276981 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | author = Livezey KB | year = 2009b | title = Range expansion of Barred Owls, part 2: facilitating ecological changes | journal = American Midland Naturalist | volume = 161 | issue = 2| pages = 323–349 | doi=10.1674/0003-0031-161.2.323| s2cid = 84426970 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Livezey | first1 = KB | year = 2010 | title = Killing barred owls to help spotted owls II: implications for many other range-expanding species | journal = Northwestern Naturalist | volume = 91 | issue = 3 | pages = 251–270 | doi = 10.1898/nwn09-38.1 | s2cid = 85425945 }}</ref> Studies indicate that thrashers that reside in the [[New England]] region of the United States during the breeding season fly toward [[the Carolinas]] and Georgia, birds located in the east of the Mississippi winter from [[Arkansas]] to Georgia, and birds located in [[the Dakotas]] and the [[Canadian Prairies|central Canadian provinces]] head towards eastern Texas and [[Louisiana]].<ref name=helm01/> When the species does migrate, it is typically for short distances and during the night.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> There are also records of the bird wintering in [[Mexico]],<ref>{{cite book|last=Peterson|first=Roger Tory|author2=L. Chalif|author3=Edward|title=A Field Guide to Mexican Birds: Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, El Salvador|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|year=1999|page=[https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetomexi00edwa/page/179 179]|url=https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetomexi00edwa|url-access=registration|isbn=978-0-395-79514-9}}</ref> as well as a [[Great Britain|British]] record of a transatlantic vagrant.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.britishbirds.co.uk/search?model=pdf&id=4200|title=Brown Thrasher in Dorset: a species new to Britain and Ireland|work=britishbirds.co.uk|access-date=22 June 2012|format=PDF}}</ref>

==Behavior==
[[File:116 Ferruginous Thrush.jpg|thumb|upright|[[John James Audubon]]'s picture depicting ''ferruginous thrush'']]
The brown thrasher has been observed either solo or in pairs. The brown thrasher is usually an elusive bird, and maintains its evasiveness with low-level flying.<ref>{{cite book|last=Brewer|first=David|title=Wrens, Dippers and Thrashers|publisher=Pica / Christopher Helm|year=2001|page=231|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-r35SXfm9BYC&pg=PA229|isbn=978-1-873403-95-2}}</ref><ref name="National Geographic11">{{cite book|last=Alderfer|first=Johnathan|title=National Geographic Backyard Guide to the Birds of North America (National Geographic Backyard Guides)|publisher=National Geographic|year=2011|page=174|isbn=978-1-4262-0720-4}}</ref> When it feels bothered, it usually hides into thickets and gives cackling calls.<ref name="National Geographic11"/> Thrashers spend most of their time on ground level or near it. When seen, it is commonly the males that are singing from unadorned branches.<ref>{{cite book|last=Brewer|first=David|title=Wrens, Dippers and Thrashers|publisher=Pica / Christopher Helm|year=2001|page=228|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-r35SXfm9BYC&pg=PA229|isbn=978-1-873403-95-2}}</ref> The brown thrasher has been noted for having an aggressive behavior,<ref>{{cite thesis|url=http://rave.ohiolink.edu/etdc/view?acc_num=osu1214492456|title=Breeding Biology and Behavior of the Brown Thrasher, (''Toxostoma rufum'')|access-date=22 June 2012|format=PDF|author=Partin H.|year=1977|work= Ph.D. Thesis, The Ohio State University, United States}}</ref> and is a staunch defender of its nest.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> However, the name does not come from attacking perceived threats, but is believed to have come from the thrashing sound the bird makes when digging through ground debris.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/><ref>{{cite book|title=Book of North American Birds: Reader's Digest Information|publisher=Reader's Digest|year=1990|page=259|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MouSwy7p0usC&pg=PA259|isbn=978-0-89577-351-7}}</ref> It is also thought that the name comes from the thrashing sound that is made while it is smashing large insects to kill and eventually eat.<ref name=IWE02>{{cite book|last=Maurice|first=Burton|author2=Burton, Robert|title=Intnernational Wildlife Encyclopedia: Volume 19|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|year=2002|page=2376|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EBl7y_MX0xoC&pg=PA2673|isbn=978-0-7614-7285-8}}</ref>

===Feeding===
This bird is [[omnivore|omnivorous]], which has a diet that includes [[insect]]s, [[Berry|berries]], [[nut (fruit)|nut]]s and [[seed]]s, as well as [[earthworm]]s, [[snail]]s, and sometimes [[lizard]]s and [[frog]]s.<ref name=Mimids12/> Across seasons and its breeding range, it was found 63% of stomach contents were made of animal matter, the remaining 37% being plant material.<ref name= Beal>{{cite book|author1=Beal, F. E. L.|author2= W. L. McAtee|author3= E. R. Kalmbach|author-link3=Edwin Richard Kalmbach| year=1916|title=Common birds of southeastern United States in relation to agriculture. U.S. Dep. Agric. Farmer's Bull. 755| publisher=USDA|page= 11|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/56848073}}</ref> During the breeding season, the diet consists primarily of [[beetle]]s, [[grasshopper]]s, and other [[arthropod]]s, and [[fruit]]s, [[nut (fruit)|nut]]s and [[seed]]s. More than 80% of the diet of brown thrasher from [[Illinois]] is made of animal matter, about 50% being beetles.<ref name= Graber>{{cite journal |author1=Graber R. R. |author2=Graber J. W. |author3=Kirk E. L. | year = 1970 | title = Illinois birds: Mimidae | journal = Ill. Nat. Hist. Surv. Biol. Notes | volume = 68 | pages = 3–38 }}</ref> In [[Iowa]], about 20% of the summer diet was found to consist of grasshoppers.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Gabrielson, I. N. |year=1912|title=A study of the home life of the Brown Thrasher, ''Toxostoma rufum'' (Linn.)|journal=Wilson Bull.|volume= 24|issue=2|pages=65–94|jstor=4154409 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/124249}}</ref> By the late summer, it begins to shift towards more of a herbivore diet, focusing on [[fruit]]s, [[nut (fruit)|nut]]s, [[seed]]s, and [[grain]]s, 60% of the food in Illinois being fruits and seeds.<ref name= Graber/><ref>{{cite book|last=Icenoggle|first=Radd|title=Birds in Place: A Habitat-Based Field Guide to the Birds of the Northern Rockies|publisher=Farcountry Press|year=2003|page=114|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3dtiZlEERCwC&pg=PA114|isbn=978-1-56037-241-7}}</ref> By winter, the customary diet of the brown thrasher is [[fruit]] and [[acorn]]s.<ref name="Nesting Behavior of the Wood Thrush">{{cite web|url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/pfw/slides/Top20_Southeast_Region_web.pdf|author=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|title=The Project FeederWatch Top 20 feeder birds in the Southeast|access-date=22 June 2012}}</ref> Wintering birds in [[Texas]] were found to eat 58% plant material (mainly [[sugar berry]] and [[poison ivy]]) and 42% animal material in October; by March, in the dry period when food supply is generally lower, 80% of the food became animal and only 20% plants.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Fischer, D. H. |year=1981|title=Wintering ecology of thrashers in southern Texas|journal= Condor |volume=83|issue=4|pages=340–346|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/103195|doi=10.2307/1367503|jstor=1367503|url-access=subscription}}</ref> [[Vertebrate]]s are only eaten occasionally and are often comprised by small [[reptile]]s and [[amphibian]]s, such as [[lizard]]s, small or young [[snake]]s, [[tree frog]]s and [[salamander]]s.<ref name= Beal/><ref>Bent, A. C. (1948). "Life histories of North American nuthatches, wrens, thrashers, and their allies". ''U.S. Natl. Mus. Bull.'' p. 195.
</ref>

The brown thrasher utilizes its vision while scouring for food. It usually forages for food under leaves, brushes, and soil debris on the ground using its bill.<ref>{{cite book|last=Mobley|first=Jason A.|title=Birds of the World|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|year=2009|page=109|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gccfdvSUTg4C&pg=PA108|isbn=978-0-7614-7775-4}}</ref> It then swipes the floor in side-to-side motions, and investigates the area it recently foraged in.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> The brown thrasher forages in a similar method to the [[long-billed thrasher]] and [[Bendire's thrasher]] (''T. longirostre'' & ''bendirei''), picking food off the ground and under [[leaf litter]], whereas thrashers with sharply decurved bills are more likely to dig into the ground to obtain food.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Engels, W. |year=1940|title=Structural adaptations in thrashers (Mimidae: genus ''Toxostoma'') with comments on interspecific relations|journal= Univ. Calif. Publ. Zool. |volume=42|pages=341–400}}</ref> Foraging success is 25% greater in dry leaf litter as compared to damp leaf letter.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Jaeger, R. G. |year=1981|title=Birds as inefficient predators on terrestrial salamanders|journal= Am. Nat. |volume=117|issue=5|pages=835–837|jstor=/2460773|doi=10.1086/283772|bibcode=1981ANat..117..835J |s2cid=85187112}}</ref> The brown thrasher can also hammer nuts such as acorns in order to remove the shell.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> It has also been noted for its flexibility in catching quick insects, as the amount of vertebrae in its neck exceeds giraffes and camels.<ref>{{cite book|last=Cruickshank|first=Allan D.|title=Cruickshank's Photographs of Birds of America: 177 Photographs and Text|publisher=Dover Publications|year=1977|page=159|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0XOlNr2pmWUC&pg=PA159|isbn=978-0-486-23497-7}}</ref> In one case, a brown thrasher was observed to dig a hole about {{convert|1.5|cm|in|abbr=on}} deep, place an acorn in it and hit the acorn until it cracked, considered to be a form of tool usage.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Hilton, B. Jr.|year= 1992|title=Tool-making and tool-using by a Brown Thrasher (''Toxostoma rufum'')|journal=Chat |volume=56|pages=4–5|url=https://www.academia.edu/4340856}}</ref> In a laboratory experiment, a brown thrasher was found to be able to discern and reject the toxic [[eastern newt]] (''Notophthalmus viridescens'') and a palatable mimic of that species, the [[red salamander]] (''Pseudotriton ruber''), but continued to eat palatable [[Desmognathus|dusky salamander]]s (''Desmognathus'' spp.).<ref>{{cite journal|author=Howard, R. R. |author2=Brodie, E. D. Jr. |year=1970|title=A mimetic relationship in salamanders: ''Notophthalmus viridescens'' and ''Pseudotriton ruber''|journal=Am. Zool. |volume=10|page=475}}</ref>

===Breeding===
[[File:Toxostoma rufum - nest 1904.jpg|thumb|right|Nest and eggs]]
Brown thrashers are typically monogamous birds, but mate-switching does occur, at times during the same season.<ref name=Mimids12/><ref name=Stockpole03>{{cite book|last=Fergus|first=Charles|title=Wildlife of Virginia and Maryland: and Washington, D.C.|publisher=Stockpole|year=2003|page=308|isbn=978-0-8117-2821-8}}</ref> Their breeding season varies by region. In the [[southeastern United States]], the breeding months begin in February and March, while May and June see the commencement of breeding in the northern portion of their breeding range. When males enter the breeding grounds, their territory can range from {{convert|2|to|10|acres|ha|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Stockpole03/><ref>{{cite book|last=Eastman|first=John|author2=Hansen, Amelia|title=Birds of Forest, Yard, and Thicket|publisher=Stockpole|year=1997|pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdsofforestyar00east/page/180 180]|isbn=978-0-8117-2680-1|url=https://archive.org/details/birdsofforestyar00east/page/180}}</ref> Around this time of the year the males are usually at their most active, singing loudly to attract potential mates, and are found on top of perches.<ref name=Mimids12>{{cite web|url=http://www.portal.state.pa.us/portal/server.pt/document/706640/mockingbirds_pdf|title=Gray Catbird, Northern Mockingbird and Brown Thrasher|access-date=3 July 2012|format=PDF}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|url=http://sora.unm.edu/node/23077|journal=The Auk|year=1977|pages=783–785|volume=94|issue=4|title=Vocal virtuosity in the Brown Thrasher|author1=Kroodsma, Donald E.  |author2=Parker, Linda D. |name-list-style=amp |doi=10.2307/4085282|jstor=4085282|doi-access=free}}</ref> The courting ritual involves the exchanging of probable nesting material. Males will sing gentler as they sight a female, and this enacts the female to grab a twig or leaf and present it to the male, with flapping wings and chirping sounds. The males might also present a gift in response and approach the female.<ref>{{cite book|last=Rylander|first=Michael K.|title=Behavior of Texas Birds: A Field Companion|publisher=University of Texas Press|year=2002|page=293|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XNdW7C_RTGAC&pg=PA294|isbn=978-0-292-77119-2}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last=Spess Jackson|first=Laura|author2=Thompson, Carol A. |author3=Dinsmore, James J. |name-list-style=amp |title=The Iowa Breeding Bird Atlas|publisher=University of Iowa Press|year=1996|page=292|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=igp_WugmoX8C&pg=PA292|isbn=978-0-87745-561-5}}</ref> Both sexes will take part in nest building once mates find each other, and will mate after the nest is completed.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/>

The female lays 3 to 5 [[Egg (biology)|egg]]s, that usually appears with a blueish or greenish tint along with reddish-brown spots.<ref name=Alberta92>{{cite book|last=Semenchuk|first=Glen Peter|title=The Atlas of Breeding Birds of Alberta|publisher=Federation of Alberta Naturalists|year=1992|page=234|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LEHmk-8V0rYC&pg=PA234|isbn=978-0-9696134-0-4}}</ref> There are rare occurrences of no spots on the eggs.<ref name=helm02/> The nest is built twiggy, lined with grass, leaves, and other forms of dead vegetation. The nests are typically built in a dense shrub or low in a tree, usually up to {{convert|2.1|m|ft|abbr=on}} high, but have built nests as high as {{convert|6|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=helm02/> They also on occasion build nests on the ground. Between eleven days to two weeks, the eggs hatch. Both parents [[incubate (bird)|incubate]] and feed the young, with the female doing most of the incubating. Nine to thirteen days after hatching, the nestlings begin to [[fledge]]. These birds raise two, sometimes even three, broods in a year.<ref>{{cite book|last=James|first=Ryan M.|title=Adirondack Wildlife: A Field Guide|publisher=University Press of New England|year=2009|page=155|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=7FI0D_YAI6QC&pg=PA155|isbn=978-1-58465-749-1}}</ref> The male sings a series of short repeated melodious phrases from an open perch to declare his territory,<ref>{{cite book|last1=Alderfer|first1=Johnathan|last2=Hess |first2=Paul|title=National Geographic Backyard Guide to the Birds of North America|publisher=National Geographic|year=2011|page=174|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oPMDsPn6fjQC&pg=PA174|isbn=978-1-58465-749-1}}</ref> and is also very aggressive in defending the nest, known to strike people and animals.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://birdvancouver.com/b_brown_thrasher.html|title=Species: Brown Thrasher Toxostoma rufum|access-date=3 July 2012|year=2012|work=Vancouver Avian Research Centre|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131104161723/http://birdvancouver.com/b_brown_thrasher.html|archive-date=4 November 2013}}</ref>

===Vocal development===
[[File:Toxostoma rufum-singing.jpg|thumb|right|Singing, [[Illinois]]]]
{{Listen|filename=Toxostoma rufum - Brown Thrasher XC136055.ogg|title=Brown thrasher song|format=[[Ogg]]}}
{{Listen
| filename     = Toxostoma rufum - Brown Thrasher XC136052.ogg
| title        = Brown thrasher song
| format       = [[Ogg]]
}}

{{Listen
| filename     = Brown Thrasher.ogg
| title        = Brown thrasher
| format       = [[Ogg]]
}}

The male brown thrasher may have the largest song repertoire of any North American bird, which has been documented as at least over 1,100 songs.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> Some sources state that each individual has up to 3,000 song phrases,<ref>{{cite book|last=Kroodsma|first=Donald|title=The Singing Life of Birds: The Art and Science of Listening to Birdsong|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|year=2005|pages=425–426|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nBI_20Buh0MC&pg=PA426|isbn=978-0-618-40568-8}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last=Stap|first=Don|title=Birdsong: A Natural History|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=2006|page=89|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LiSFKZEnMg8C|isbn=978-0-19-530901-0}}</ref> while others put the number beyond 3,000.<ref>{{cite web|title=All About Birds|url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/AllAboutBirds/studying/birdsongs/vocaldev|publisher=Cornell University|access-date=26 June 2012}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Thrasher|url=https://www.birds.cornell.edu/AllAboutBirds/studying/birdsongs/vocaldev|publisher=TropicalBirds.com|access-date=26 June 2012}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Brown Thrasher|url=http://www.outdooralabama.com/watchable-wildlife/what/Birds/tmt/bt.cfm|publisher=Outdoor Alabama|access-date=26 June 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120530013506/http://www.outdooralabama.com/watchable-wildlife/what/Birds/tmt/bt.cfm|archive-date=30 May 2012}}</ref> The males' singing voice usually contains more of a melodic tone than that of the related [[grey catbird]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Peterson|first=Roger Tory|title=A Field Guide to the Birds of Texas: And Adjacent States|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|year=1998|page=259|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=83pYrPU6jh0C&pg=SL26-PA59|isbn=978-0-19-530901-0}}</ref> Its song are coherent phrases that are iterated no more than three times, but has been done for minutes at a time.<ref name=helm02/><ref>{{cite journal|url=http://sora.unm.edu/node/20421|title=The Monotony Threshold in Singing Birds|journal=The Auk|volume=77|issue=2|pages=176–192|author=Hartshorne, Charles|doi=10.2307/4081470|jstor=4081470|year=1956|doi-access=free}}</ref> By the fall, the male sings with smoother sub-songs.<ref name=helm02/> During the winter, the males may also sing in short spurts during altercations with neighboring males.<ref name="Nesting Behavior of the Wood Thrush"/>

In the birds' youth, ''alarm noises'' are the sounds made.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> As an adult, the brown thrasher has an array of sounds it will make in various situations. Both male and females make ''smack'' and ''teeooo''-like alarm calls when provoked, and ''hijjj'' sounds at dusk and dawn.<ref>{{cite book|last=Lang|first=Elliot|title=Know Your Bird Sounds: Songs and calls of yard, garden, and city birds|publisher=Stackpole|year=2004|page=35|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ChyiBZr6GU8C&pg=PA35|isbn=978-0-8117-2963-5}}</ref> Others calls may consist of an acute, sudden ''chakk'',<ref name=helm02/> ''rrrrr'', a ''Tcheh'' sound in the beginning that ends with an ''eeeur'', ''kakaka'', and sounds reminiscent of a stick scraping a concrete sidewalk.<ref>{{cite book|last=Dunne|first=Pete|title=Pete Dunne's Essential Field Guide Companion|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|year=2006|pages=[https://archive.org/details/petedunnesessent00dunn/page/508 508]–509|url=https://archive.org/details/petedunnesessent00dunn|url-access=registration|isbn=978-0-618-23648-0}}</ref> Brown thrashers are noted for their mimicry (as a member of the family ''Mimidae''), but they are not as diverse in this category as their relative the [[northern mockingbird]].<ref name=helm02/><ref name=IWE02/> However, during the breeding season, the mimicking ability of the male is at its best display, impersonating sounds from [[tufted titmice]] (''Baeolophus bicolor''), [[northern cardinal]]s (''Cardinalis cardinalis''), wood thrushes, [[northern flicker]]s (''Colaptes auratus''), among other species.<ref name=Stockpole03/>

==Predation and threats==
Although this bird is widespread and still common, it has declined in numbers in some areas due to loss of suitable [[habitat (ecology)|habitat]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.mibirdatlas.org/Portals/12/MBA2010/BRTHaccount.pdf|title=Brown Thrasher (Toxostoma rufum) – Michigan Bird Atlas|access-date=8 July 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160311191655/http://mibirdatlas.org/portals/12/mba2010/brthaccount.pdf|archive-date=11 March 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.conservewildlifenj.org/species/fieldguide/view/Toxostoma%20rufum/|title=New Jersey Endangered and Threatened Species Field Guide |access-date=2023-04-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140508223616/http://www.conservewildlifenj.org/species/fieldguide/view/Toxostoma%20rufum/ |archive-date=2014-05-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://ny.audubon.org/sites/default/files/documents/hrvc_brownthrasher.pdf|title=BROWN THRASHER (''Toxostoma rufum''): Guidance for Conservation|access-date=8 July 2012|work=audubon.org|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140508222818/http://ny.audubon.org/sites/default/files/documents/hrvc_brownthrasher.pdf|archive-date=8 May 2014}}</ref> Despite the decrease, the rate does not warrant a status towards [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]].<ref>{{cite web|author=BirdLife International|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/22711099|title=LC: Brown Thrasher ''Toxostoma rufum''|work= IUCN Red List for birds|year=2012 |access-date=26 July 2012}}</ref> One of the natural nuisances is the parasitic [[brown-headed cowbird]] (''Molothrus ater''), but these incidents are rare. Whenever these situations occur, the brown thrashers usually discard the cowbirds' eggs.<ref name="Cornell"/><ref name="Observation and Experiment in the Analysis of Interactions between Brood Parasites and Their Hosts">{{Cite journal | doi = 10.1086/282702|jstor=/2459388| title = Observation and Experiment in the Analysis of Interactions between Brood Parasites and Their Hosts| journal = The American Naturalist| volume = 105| issue = 941| pages = 71–74| year = 1971| last1 = Rothstein | first1 = S. I. |bibcode=1971ANat..105...71R |s2cid=84032938}}</ref> Occasionally, the thrasher has thrown out their own eggs instead of the cowbird eggs due to similar egg size,<ref name=Stackpole97/> and at least one recorded event raised a fledgling.<ref name="Observation and Experiment in the Analysis of Interactions between Brood Parasites and Their Hosts"/> Northern cardinals and grey catbirds are also major competitors for thrashers in terms of territorial gain.<ref name=Stackpole97>{{cite book|last=Eastman|first=John|author2=Hansen, Amelia|title=Birds of Forest, Yard, and Thicket|publisher=Stackpole|year=1997|pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdsofforestyar00east/page/181 181]|isbn=978-0-8117-2680-1|url=https://archive.org/details/birdsofforestyar00east/page/181}}</ref> Because of the apparent lack of opportunistic behavior around species like these, thrashers are prone to be driven out of zones for territory competition.<ref name=Stackpole12>{{cite book|last=Eastman|first=John|title=The Eastman Guide to Birds|publisher=Stackpole|year=2012|page=181|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4s039PKpc4kC&pg=PA181|isbn=978-1-81174-522-9}}</ref> Brown thrashers have tendencies to double-brood or have failures on their first nesting attempts due to predation.<ref name=Stackpole12p180>{{cite book|last=Eastman|first=John|title=The Eastman Guide to Birds|publisher=Stackpole|year=2012|page=180|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4s039PKpc4kC&pg=PA181|isbn=978-1-81174-522-9}}</ref> Grey catbirds have been seen invading brown thrashers' nests and breaking their eggs.<ref name="University of Michigan Museum of Zoology"/> Other than the catbird, [[snake]]s, [[birds of prey]], and [[Domestic cats|cats]] are among the top predators of the thrasher.<ref name="Facts about Brown Thrasher: Encyclopedia of Life">{{cite web |url=http://eol.org/pages/1050708/details |title=Facts about Brown Thrasher: Encyclopedia of Life |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111016201527/http://eol.org/pages/1050708/overview |archive-date=2011-10-16 |access-date=2023-04-05}}</ref> In [[Kansas]], at least eight species of snake were identified as potentially serious sources of nest failure.<ref>Cavitt, J. F. (1998). The role of food supply and nest predation in limiting reproductive success of Brown Thrashers (''Toxostoma rufum''): effects of predator removal, food supplements and predation risk. Phd Thesis. Kansas State Univ. Manhattan.</ref> Among the identified avian predators of adults are [[Cooper's hawk]]s (''Accipiter cooperii''),<ref>{{cite journal|author=Toland, B. |year=1985|title=Food habits and hunting success of Cooper's Hawks in Missouri|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|volume=56|issue=4 (Autumn)|pages= 419–422|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/51224}}</ref> [[northern goshawk]] (''Accipiter gentilis''),<ref>Curnutt, J. (2007). Conservation Assessment for Northern Goshawk (''Accipiter gentilis'') Linnaeus in the Western Great Lakes. USDA Forest Service. 105 pp.</ref> [[broad-winged hawk]]s (''Buteo platypterus''),<ref>{{cite journal|author=Burns, F. L. |year=1911|title=A monograph of the Broad-winged Hawk (''Buteo platypterus'')|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume= 23|issue=3–4|pages=143–320|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/3120}}</ref> [[Merlin (bird)|merlin]]s (''Falco columbarius''),<ref>{{cite journal|author=Sodhi, N. S.|author2=Oliphant, L. W. |year=1993|title=Prey selection by urban-breeding merlins|journal= The Auk|pages= 727–735|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/25304|jstor=4088628|volume=110|issue=4 |doi=10.2307/4088628|url-access=subscription}}</ref> [[peregrine falcon]]s (''Falco peregrinus''),<ref>Ward, F. P., & Laybourne, R. C. (1985). [http://www.raptors-international.org/book/conservation_studies_on_raptors_1985/Ward_Laybourne_1985_303-309.pdf  ''A difference in prey selection by adult and immature peregrine falcons during autumn migration'']. Conservation studies on raptors. Page Brothers, Norwich, England, pp. 303–309.</ref> [[eastern screech-owl]]s (''Megascops asio''),<ref>{{Cite journal | doi = 10.3996/nafa.71.0001| title = The Screech Owl: Its Life History and Population Ecology in Northern Ohio| journal = North American Fauna| volume = 71| pages = 1–65| year = 1975| last1 = Vancamp | first1 = L. F. | last2 = Henny | first2 = C. J. | url = https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/pdfs/ADA323345.pdf| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20170924022318/http://www.dtic.mil/get-tr-doc/pdf?AD=ADA323345| url-status = live| archive-date = September 24, 2017}}</ref> [[great horned owl]]s (''Bubo virginianus'')<ref>Murphy, R. K. (1997). [http://www.nrs.fs.fed.us/pubs/5499 Importance of prairie wetlands and avian prey to breeding Great Horned Owls (''Bubo virginianus'') in northwestern North Dakota].  U.S. Dept. of Agriculture, Forest Service, North Central Forest Experiment Station, pp. 286–298.</ref> [[barred owl]]s (''Strix varia'')<ref name= Errington>{{cite journal|author=Errington, P. L. |year=1932|title=Food habits of southern Wisconsin raptors. Part I. Owls|journal= Condor|pages= 176–186|jstor=1363563|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/97944|volume=34|issue=4|doi=10.2307/1363563|url-access=subscription}}</ref> and [[long-eared owl]]s (''Asio otus'').<ref name= Errington/>

The brown thrasher methods of defending itself include using its bill, which can inflict significant damage to species smaller than it, along with wing-flapping and vocal expressions.<ref name="Facts about Brown Thrasher: Encyclopedia of Life"/>

==State bird==
The brown thrasher is the [[List of U.S. state birds|state bird]] of [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]].<ref>Official Code of Georgia § 50-3-50</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Georgia State Symbols |url=https://www.libs.uga.edu/capitolmuseum/gainfo/symbols.html |access-date=19 June 2024 |website=Georgia Capitol Museum |via=University of Georgia Libraries |archive-date=8 January 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230108192110/https://www.libs.uga.edu/capitolmuseum/gainfo/symbols.html }}</ref> The brown thrasher also was the inspiration for the name of [[Atlanta]]'s former [[National Hockey League]] team, the [[Atlanta Thrashers]],<ref>{{Cite news |title=History of the NHL's Thrashers in Atlanta |url=https://www.ajc.com/sports/hockey/history-the-nhl-thrashers-atlanta/2j0xymYxqCdprzzO3KZp4I/ |access-date=19 June 2024 |work=The Atlanta Journal-Constitution}}</ref> who relocated in 2011 to become the current [[Winnipeg Jets]] (the original [[Winnipeg Jets (1972-1996)|Jets]] relocated to [[Phoenix, Arizona|Phoenix]] in 1996 to become the [[Arizona Coyotes|Coyotes]]).<ref>{{Cite news |date=31 May 2011 |title=Winnipeg's hockey history |url=https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/winnipeg-s-hockey-history-1.1114449 |access-date=19 June 2024 |work=Canadian Broadcasting Corporation News}}</ref> The Coyotes have since relocated again and are now known as the [[Utah Mammoth]].
